thread_tweets.full_text: According to the recent NYT article, the person in the administration most against releasing the Epstein files is Trump, who'd have to resign if the allegations in there are true, and the person most in favor of releasing them is Vance, who'd become president if they're true.

thread_tweets.text:
So Occam's Razor says: at least some of the allegations about Trump in the Epstein files are in fact true.

Presumably this is why Elon mentioned it when they had that falling out. It would have been a pretty weak claim to say he's in the files, but none of it is true.

tweets.text:
Since late January 2026:

#Gold: -22% 
#Silver: -45% 
#Uranium LT price: +5.6%

Gold and silver are still in powerful long-term bull markets imo, but it's notable that uranium has continued moving higher while many uranium equities have moved lower.
Therefore, I believe we are soon to see a big rebound in uranium equities.

$FUU should be one of those. The share price has been badly hit, but remember, this is the team behind 4 discoveries, nobody beats that. Will be very interesting to see how the Tetra Zone develops.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with F3 Uranium. This is not financial advice. Please do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
Look at this daily volume.

Everyone knows IPO season will mint fortunes for visionary founders, strong companies, and their early investors, while quietly extracting billions from retail.

But the single biggest winner of this IPO season isn’t going public at all.

It’s Hyperliquid. The only venue where the entire world can trade these listings 24/7, permissionlessly, from day one. SpaceX today is just the preview, and once again it was priced almost perfectly, while everyone was trying to predict it with their spreadsheets.

While TradFi celebrates its IPOs, Hyperliquid is quietly onboarding all of TradFi onchain without them even realizing it.

Hyperliquid.

Bill was an important player in shaping Blockchain policy in DC/Consensys

Excited for him to be joining us at @RoboStrategy to lead our government policy work. The US doesn’t yet have a National robotics strategy, but it will soon and getting it right is important for the future of the country from both an economic, defense, and supply chain independence point of view.

We hope to bring the founders of the Robotics industry to the table so they can share their experiences and views with law makers and federal agencies on how the government can empower the growth of the domestic industry

• National

thread_tweets.text:
Price Discovery in Crypto

Everyone says HL displaced Binance for price discovery. Arrakis ran a lead-lag study across 29 assets to check.

Liquidity leader and price leader turned out to be two different venues.

thread_tweets.text:
Is FDV wrong lens for HYPE?

On FDV, it's a $70B token. Against a $16B market cap and ~$41B outstanding TC, that number feels invented.

Nobody values equities on maximum possible supply. If the FDV is the wrong lens, then HYPE is a lot cheaper than what crypto natives assume.
thread_tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
DeFi Monk explains why Hyperliquid shouldn’t be valued using FDV

“Right now we think it should probably be somewhere in between, if not closer to circulating”

thread_tweets.text:
Morpho Midnight Deep Dive

It's the new fixed-rate, fixed-maturity credit primitive where lenders and borrowers trade fungible “units” that behave like zero-coupon bonds.

This walks through how it works. Clear enough even if you're not technical.

thread_tweets.text:
Potential of Pre-IPO instruments

The most valuable growth phase of a company now happens entirely in private markets. Retail's been locked out for a decade.

A new sector is racing to fill that gap.

But there are a lot of traps you need to be mindful of

tweets.full_text:
The best crypto research this week had one thing in common...

It disagreed with the crowd.

Price discovery, HYPE's valuation, agentic payments are challenged with actual data.

Here are the 8 best articles I read before the crowd catches up:

Price Discovery in Crypto

Everyone says HL displaced Binance for price discovery. Arrakis ran a lead-lag study across 29 assets to check.

Liquidity leader and price leader turned out to be two different venues.

Is FDV wrong lens for HYPE?

On FDV, it's a $70B token. Against a $16B market cap and ~$41B outstanding TC, that number feels invented.

Nobody values equities on maximum possible supply. If the FDV is the wrong lens, then HYPE is a lot cheaper than what crypto natives assume.

Build your own onchain analyst

0xJeff shows how to configure Hermes (an AI analyst agent) as an onchain forensics tool that traces wallet activity, identifies who bought/dumped tokens, and detects whale accumulation.

Great article.

Morpho Midnight Deep Dive

It's the new fixed-rate, fixed-maturity credit primitive where lenders and borrowers trade fungible “units” that behave like zero-coupon bonds.

This walks through how it works. Clear enough even if you're not technical.

Potential of Pre-IPO instruments

The most valuable growth phase of a company now happens entirely in private markets. Retail's been locked out for a decade.

A new sector is racing to fill that gap.

But there are a lot of traps you need to be mindful of

Truth About Agentic Payments

Everyone claims it'll take crypto to Valhalla.

Here's the real picture from someone who actually built in the sector. Spoiler alert: the hyped metrics are largely misleading.
https://t.co/8rshCMrtS9

tweets.text:
𝗪𝗵𝗼 𝗠𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗱 𝗠𝘆 𝗘𝗱𝗴𝗲?

Most investors spend 95% of their time analyzing numbers.

Revenue growth.
Margins.
Guidance.
Valuation.

The problem is that everyone can see the same numbers.

Twenty years ago, investors like Warren Buffett could build an edge by reading financial statements better than almost everyone else.

Information moved slowly. Data was expensive. Analysis was limited.

Ten years ago, investors like Bill Ackman were still generating outsized returns through deep fundamental research, activism, and understanding businesses better than the market.

Today, every filing is instantly available to everyone.

Hedge funds, analysts, retail investors, and now AI systems can process the same information within minutes.

The market evolved.
Information evolved.
AI evolved.

Investors who didn’t evolve underperformed.

Even Bill Ackman’s most famous trade wasn’t finding a hidden line item in a balance sheet. It was recognizing a risk the market was largely ignoring and buying protection before COVID.

The edge moved.

I haven’t used Excel or a calculator in over 10 years.

Yet during that time I have outperformed the market by thousands of percentage points.

Not because numbers don’t matter.

But because numbers alone rarely provide an edge anymore.

If you’re only chasing valuations, you’re chasing information everybody already knows.

You can scroll through X all day and see endless posts saying:

“Bitcoin is so cheap.”

“PayPal is so cheap.”

And my personal favorite:

“This is the cheapest valuation in the company’s history.”

I probably see 10 posts like that every day.

So what?

Everybody can see the same valuation metrics.

Everybody knows the stock is trading at 8x earnings, 1x sales, or whatever ratio is being promoted that day.

If the opportunity is obvious to everyone, why would that be an edge?

In fact, some of the cheapest stocks get even cheaper.

The question isn’t whether something looks cheap.

The question is: what does the market believe that makes it cheap, and what is the market getting wrong?

One of my favorite edges is understanding the actions of the people who know a business best.

Not blindly following them.

Understanding why they’re doing what they’re doing.

In my latest three recommendations, $STAA, $WGS, and $IMDX, the numbers weren’t particularly attractive. Analysts were negative, and the last quarters weren’t great.

Yet within a few months, all three were up between 50% and 100%.

Almost nobody was talking about them.

You couldn’t scroll through X and find endless threads about how cheap they were.

At the same time, some of the people who knew these businesses best were buying aggressively.

That’s where I started paying attention.

Not because someone bought.

Because I wanted to understand why they bought.

There are entire funds and ETFs built around insider buying.

They scan thousands of companies, track insider transactions, apply statistical models, and buy based on those signals.

And they’re not wrong.

But that’s still only one piece of the puzzle.

Insider buying is not the thesis.

It’s one facet of the diamond.

It’s a clue.

The real work starts after you see the purchase, not before.

Who is buying?

How much are they buying?

What do they know?

What are their incentives?

Why are they acting now instead of six months ago?

Those are the questions that matter.

If you read the hedge fund letters on $STAA, you understood the thesis.

If you listened to the conference calls on $WGS and paid attention after insiders committed roughly $100 million of their own capital, you understood where the opportunity was.

Same thing with $IMDX.

The market is very good at pricing today’s numbers.

It’s much less efficient at pricing human behavior, incentives, and conviction.

tweets.text:
Saylor is basically explaining that when convertible bonds and preferred shares are introduced in the balance sheet, there is no objective and unquestionable definition of the mNAV.

People must have their own expectations about BTC future price action to judge by themselves if an operation is dilutive or accretive for shareholders. Sometimes a decision can be short-terme dilutive but long-term accretive.

STRC is back at $97, two days after ticking around $90.5

Third time this happens during a significant BTC drawdown.

The MAIN factor that is moving STRC price is BTC price, by at least an order of magnitude.

Basically STRC holders start panicking when BTC goes down too much, https://t.co/6bSqYJ7jj7
tweets.full_text:
Interesting to notice that SATA, the STRC-like product of Strive, is currently trading at $99 again, while STRC is still at $96, and despite having a similar leverage level as Strategy.

SATA has slightly higher dividend rate and is going to pay dividends daily.

tweets.text:
One basic but useful risk management advice is: reduce size on almost all of your trades

Oversizing leads to being a prey for the variance of the crypto market, which makes you buy high and sell low

tweets.text:
I think the vision for content and talks were on point. 

Main issue was mostly ticket & hotel price. Expensive for locals to attend and for people to fly in. I spoke with folks who paid >$300 a night and I myself only stayed 2 nights due to hotel costs. 

Also it’s the first iteration; lots of confs are smaller but then grow over time.
